title: Difference Between JVM, JRE, and JDK
body:
Understanding the Java platform stack is essential for every Java developer.

**JVM (Java Virtual Machine)** — the runtime engine that executes Java bytecode. It is platform-specific (Windows JVM, Linux JVM, etc.) but bytecode is platform-neutral. The JVM handles memory management, garbage collection, and security sandboxing.

**JRE (Java Runtime Environment)** — the JVM plus the standard class libraries needed to *run* Java applications. End users who only need to run Java programs install the JRE.

**JDK (Java Development Kit)** — the JRE plus development tools: the `javac` compiler, `javadoc` documentation generator, `jar` archiver, `jshell` REPL, debugger, and more. Developers install the JDK.

The relationship is:
```
JDK ⊇ JRE ⊇ JVM
```

Since Java 11 the distinction between JRE and JDK is largely collapsed — Oracle no longer ships a separate JRE download. The `jlink` tool lets you build a custom minimal runtime image containing only the modules your application needs.

examples.label: Check your Java version

title: Stack Memory and Heap Space in Java
body:
The JVM divides memory into two key regions: the **stack** and the **heap**.

**Stack Memory** stores:
- Method call frames (local variables, parameters, return addresses)
- Primitive values declared inside methods
- References (pointers) to heap objects

Each thread has its own stack. When a method is called a new frame is pushed; when the method returns the frame is popped. Stack memory is fast but limited — deep recursion causes a `StackOverflowError`.

**Heap Space** stores:
- All objects created with `new`
- Instance variables of those objects
- Static variables (in a special method area)

The heap is shared across all threads and is managed by the **Garbage Collector (GC)**, which reclaims memory from objects that are no longer reachable. The heap is divided into **Young Generation** (Eden + Survivor spaces) and **Old Generation** to optimize GC performance.

Understanding this split helps you diagnose `OutOfMemoryError`, tune JVM flags like `-Xms`, `-Xmx` (heap size) and `-Xss` (stack size), and write GC-friendly code.

examples.label: Stack vs Heap illustration

title: Class Loaders in Java
body:
The **ClassLoader** subsystem is responsible for loading `.class` bytecode files into the JVM at runtime. Java uses a **delegation model**: each class loader first asks its parent before trying to load a class itself.

**Built-in class loaders (Java 9+):**
- **Bootstrap ClassLoader** — loads core Java classes (`java.lang.*`, `java.util.*`) from the JDK modules. It is written in native code and has no Java parent.
- **Platform ClassLoader** (was Extension CL) — loads platform/extension classes.
- **Application (System) ClassLoader** — loads classes from the application classpath (`-cp` / `CLASSPATH`).

**Loading phases:**
1. **Loading** — reads bytecode and creates a `Class` object
2. **Linking** — verifies bytecode, prepares static fields, resolves symbolic references
3. **Initialization** — runs static initializers and assigns static field values

You can write a custom `ClassLoader` by extending `java.lang.ClassLoader` and overriding `findClass()`. This enables hot-reloading, plugin systems, and loading classes from non-standard locations (database, network, encrypted jar).

examples.label: Inspecting class loaders
